speaker: interviewer
text: Roll number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon, you were telling us that he said at the station--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it that he said?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, the-- the officer, we have nothing to push to come to Dabie, because all the people in Dabie are gonna come here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you arrived in Dabie?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then a couple days later, I arrived to Dabie. I smuggled the [INAUDIBLE] through, and I went to Dabie. And I came home and I met my family. I was home about three or four months, and then I was taken to a labor camp. Sent away to labor camp, to Posen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it happen that you were the one in the family that was taken to the labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the other two kids were too young. They were only 13 years old. And my father, I-- they told-- I don't want to-- they decided-- the-- the city, the Jewish Federation had to decide who should go and who shouldn't go. They forced them do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one from the federation by us, he said because I have to say who have to go, I'll go myself to this. And he went. He didn't live, though. He was killed there in the camp. So he said, listen, Leon, if-- I know you came from camp just. You was out a year in camp and was in the war. You don't supposed to go. But we have to put on so many people, and we have to take at least one from each family. If you don't want to go, your father has to go. So I say OK, I go. What was the name of the labor camp? Do you remember where it was located?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hart was the labor camp, the name of the camp. Near Posen-- Poznan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The condition was terrible. We have to go up in the morning, they gave you a small piece of bread. And you had to march 25 kilometers to the job, to the work. And there, you have to work hard. And not only the hard labor, if they decide, the Germans who are staying there and watching you, that you don't work good enough, so they had poles put on with hooks in the back in the top. Tied up your hands and hang you on the hook. There's a lot, you hang a whole day, they were finished with you. When they-- oh, it was kids, 15 years, 16 years, 18 years, the oldest with maybe 23, 24. So they took a kid like this, they hanged him up. He hollered, maybe, an hour. And after an hour he couldn't holler anymore. He was finished. We had to carry him home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this camp I was a year. But I didn't go to work for a year. I was to work only two weeks. Then the leader of the camp-- he was a Gestapo person. He was in civil clothing, he didn't put on any uniform. And he made me for the tailor of the camp, I suppose to fix the clothing for the people what were there in camp, that they shouldn't turn up to work. But I didn't do it. He brought me in material to make suits for him, for his wife, and he brought friends from the city. I sewed for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the boys went to work and they told the Germans that I am home and I don't-- they we're jealous, you see, that I don't do the work what I supposed to do. I do nothing, I'm just sitting there and having a good time. So the Germans said there, we're going to bring him down here and he don't gonna survive one day. He said, we'll do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they called up to the leader of the camp. So he said to them, you have the leadership there, and I have the leadership here. I do whatever I want, and you do whatever you want there. And he going to stay here as long I am the leader of the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of this SS leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of what?

speaker: interviewer
text: The SS leader who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I should remember him. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] He was from Frankfurt am Main, I know. And the name, I should remember the name. But right now, I can't make it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive word from home during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: How?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear from your family in Dabie during this year?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had only one-- once we heard from Dabie that all the people were killed. Somebody wrote a letter from another town. And they were all taken to Chelmno, only five kilometers from town, and they were all killed there. And nobody survived. And so, well, we figured, they're killing everybody, so they're don't going to better with us. Oh, his name was Bouman.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-O-U, probably, M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe that your family had been killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, I couldn't believe. But then when I heard from another town, and from another. But of course, with boys living in different towns that write to them, so. They had to write also with such words, that they-- because the leadership in the camps that read the letters, else they shouldn't understand what they write. Otherwise they would be in trouble, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you how the people were killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they couldn't explain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I did find out. They took them on the town in a-- in a cluster, like a church. And there they made them dress up, all the clothing leaving. And they pushed them by force, and beating, to a truck who was staying outside by the door, with the-- with the down-- how goes it? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're putting down, to be able to go up. And they push them up on that, and then they lift it up and close the truck. A ramp, you know? And as they closed up the trucks, they drove to the forest. And they put in gas in the truck, and these that came out to the forest have all been dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was made ditches in the beginning, and they throw them in the ditches. They was half dead. They throw them into the ditches. And people what made the ditches did see them, they recognize everybody. They were from the same town. And-- and then they said it took them too long time to put them in ditches. They can't make so many ditches, so many people they're bringing down, in the thousands. So start to bring machines, crematorium, where they burned them there. They gassed them the same way, in the trucks, and they burned them there. And by the end when they run away, the Germans, they opened up all the ditches and they burned everything. There shouldn't be a mark, because they know the Russians is going to come in, they're going to open them up and show for the world what they did. So burned everything. I was there now, three years ago, in that forest. It's made cemeteries with signs of how many Jewish people was killed there, and how many kids. And they brought them from all over Poland, they brought them from France, they brought them from all different countries. It's, I think, 600,000 was killed there. In two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in your first labor camp, at Hart?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a year, I think. And from there they took us to  a different labor camp, near Bydgoszcz. And that was, the name of the camp-- there was a small, little town. Oh, it's out of my mind right now, the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was this camp like?

speaker: interviewee
text: If not that Jewish leader we had there, the camp would be a good camp. No, he made it-- destroyed everything. And we came 100 people there, and it didn't took more than a half a year from the 100 was only left 40. He killed them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this Jewish leader kill them?

speaker: interviewee
text: He killed them. He just killed them. One ran away-- an older person, he ran away. And it wasn't two days he was in the camp. He went-- the police control over us was the burgermeister. You know, the-- like the-- the mayor from town. And the mayor was a nice guy. So he went to the mayor and say, you have to go and let know the Gestapo right away, because otherwise they're going to hang you. He made the mayor frightened. I know out west in the other camp he's hiding. So the mayor said, we're going to wait another two days. Maybe he going to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then the police someplace else caught him. Where are you going? He said he's from that camp. So why did you run away? He said, I was very hungry so I was going to look for food. So they took him in and they gave him a good dinner. And the mayor, he sent out a horse with wagon to bring him back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he came to the camp we was working, so he killed him right away. He took an axe, he came over the head and throw him into the grave, he wasn't yet dead. Somebody was in the camp what saw it. That guy what was in that camp which saw this, he is still alive. He's here-- Miami.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of this Jewish leader? Was he a kapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: His first name was Sheol. Did you hear the name Sheol? Sheol. And the last name was Lubrenetsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You saw the tape, no? In the tape that shows you, we hanged him during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who hanged him?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of us what were survived in the camp. That was in the same time when there was the uprising in Poland, in Warsaw, in the ghetto, the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to go out there to the meister there. And I sewed for him clothing in place for working there in the black work. So I worked for him. So I told him everything what's happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they didn't know why the people dying out, so many. They're getting to eat, they're getting food, and everybody dies, he say. So I told him why. They're not dying, they're killing them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told the mayor. And the mayor was a nice guy, too. And he came and asked us why, oh, it's true. So he came down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he came out and show him that everybody is here and they give a report to the mayor, the mayor said to him, you [GERMAN], you pig. I don't want to talk you anymore. You go to the wall there, and stay with your face to the wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left that camp in '43 when we went to Auschwitz. Right after that, what we killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- they came, the Germans, and there was uprising in Warsaw. And they took all the small camps and took them all to Auschwitz. There was all around, many camps that girls and boys-- was many camps around there. How did you feel when you arrived at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what Auschwitz is! I never heard about Auschwitz when I was in the camp, until 1943. I didn't know nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive at Auschwitz? We arrived with the train in Auschwitz. And as we arrived, in the middle of the night, they took us all to get out, and made light with reflectors. And right away, this one on this side, this on this side. I didn't know what it means. Do you remember the date when you got there? The month and the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know '43, about July. About July. Or July or June. I think it was July.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you, right away they made two lines. One in this side, one-- who so is older went right to the crematorium. And who young boys-- like, I was that time about 25, so they put me in the side to work. And they put a full-- We know already they're not taking him to the crematorium. Because one guy on the way, on the train, told us what he know about Auschwitz. And he say, tomorrow morning there'll be nothing from us. Everybody will be killed. He said to us, we should take off the floor and lower us down. Run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that night, they took us on a truck, all the healthy, young boys on a truck. And the truck was covered with a, how goes it? No. A cloth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were so tied up, we figured, they're putting gas here in the truck. So we took knives, whoever had them, we opened up-- [LAUGHS] and they drove away with us. And the other people they took to the crematorium. And they drove away with us about five or six kilometers from that there. There was a department from Auschwitz. And that name of this camp was Buna-- if you heard about it. A few kilometers from Auschwitz. And we went in this camp. They locked us in a shower, and we thought, now they're going to get us, really, like the guy told us. But we got a shower. [LAUGHS] We weren't gassed. We came out. They took every-- we had clothing, yeah, but they took away everything. And even I had food yet-- what I was working, what that guy there, what he gave me everything. The German guy, because I sewed for him things, and they gave me-- so I had everything with me. And I had to throw away everything when I came. Gave up everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went out, without anything. No food, no clothing, no nothing. They gave us those striped uniforms. And the next day they took us to work. Were you given a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. On-- on the next day in the morning. Maybe there was another day was in the camp, and then we get into work. I don't remember exactly. They gave us numbers, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your number?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The newcomers what came in got the worst job there. So was always, for newcomers. So first of all, we were sleeping not in barracks, there was not enough barracks. They had a tent, a very big tent with three pritches, one on the top of the other. And with about 500 people under one tent. In the morning-- it was still dark, it maybe was 5 o'clock-- in the dark-- they throw us outside. And outside where we're staying-- that was in winter-- are lined with sinks. And go wash yourself, with cold water from the sink. And then you put back on that-- that jacket, with the pants you had, and you were staying outside. They gave you the piece of bread. It was freezing. We used to knock one the other with our backs to warm up. In the morning, they took us to work. We had to march to the music while we went out the gate for the camp. The music was playing, we had to march after the music.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was playing the music?

speaker: interviewee
text: They played that march, so that you-- you should work good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a-- a taped music, or was there an orchestra?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, an orchestra. All prisoners orchestra. So we came out to the factory. There they had a chemical factory they build that time in Poland. That was in [NON-ENGLISH], Auschwitz. We came out to the factory. So we had to go and we got jobs, everything, we helped to build the factory. There was many buildings they was building. So I was working the kommando what there was-- had to make-- they made ditches very deep. And then you have to go down in the ditches and put down telephone wires around before they build the building.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a big wheel with a heavy cable. They put-- if you saw you know what's happened. So they cut-- they-- a-- a cable like this, also-- when the lady fell under the wheel. So we had to go down, 400, 500 people, and carry that cable on their-- and they was going with whips, leather whips, eh! Take it, take it! And somebody was shorter, he couldn't reach, so I had to carry for him. Who was there with whips?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the kapos-- no. Mostly Germans, I think, were there. Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jewish kapos there, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Jewish kapos, yeah. And-- and they were-- if you put in-- the ground down was so soft, and you had wooden shoes what was maybe a half a yard too big, and you walked in with that wooden shoe in that ground. So when you took out your leg, your foot without a shoe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were still beating you up, all day long. And I figured, how long can a person survive like this? Not long. In that time, I got sick and I went to the hospital in Auschwitz. What kind of food were you getting?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning you get a piece of bread. And I think they gave a little black coffee, something like water. And on-- in the factory it was lunchtime, they gave you a soup, which was just water, hot water. If you find a piece of potato, you were lucky. [LAUGHS] And in the evening when you come home they gave you a soup again. The soup was the same way. When you went to the soup-- so one guy, what was the leader in the camp, he gave out the soup. If he knows you, he put that big spoon down to the bottom, there was a few potatoes. When he didn't know you, he gave you the water from the top, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive any news from the outside world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Only news we could receive if they came people in-- new people came in, they brought in. They brought in new people from France, from Italy. Jewish people. So we got a little news from them, what's going on. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to follow the Jewish religious calendar?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no religion there. There was nothing. There was no time for anything, anybody. The whole thing there was to get a piece of bread, that's all. That's all you worry about.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the Red Cross? Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't come, any Red Cross didn't come. Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What provisions did they have for illness?

speaker: interviewee
text: For what?

speaker: interviewer
text: In case you got sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you got sick and you feel you are sick, you had the right to go to the hospital in evening after supper. Because you had nothing to do. But you could go down to the hospital, there was a hospital. And when you came to the hospital, if you could explain for the doctor, and the doctor recognize you really need to go to the hospital, he took you in. But when you come to the hospital you don't survive, because Mengele come in every-- twice a week. And whoever was laying in hospital more than three days, he took him to the-- to Auschwitz to the crematorium. You had no way to stay in the hospital, because you knew if you stay in the hospital, they're going to take you away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you when you said you began to get sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got sick, so I figured this way. I am four years already in camp, and I don't see it should come to an end, that that war should end one day. And I don't want to live anymore. I don't want to live. Let them send me to Auschwitz, let them burn me. I can't take it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: You understand? We were beaten up every day. I couldn't take. So I say, I go down to the hospital. If the doctor take me in, fine. Let them send me Auschwitz, to the crematorium. I didn't want to live. I know my family is not alive anymore. It's only I am by myself. And I have to-- to-- this kind of work, and this kind of living,

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have to fight to survive. And I will not survive anyhow. I think no one gonna survive. We didn't know nothing, if it's-- if the war comes to an end. It was 19-- it must have been in 1944 in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I talked this way to the doctor. The doctor was a prisoner, too. So he says to me, don't talk to my conscience. I can't help you. I'm a prisoner like you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, do to me what I want. So he did. He gave me a paper I shouldn't go to work the next day. That was enough not to go to work. And now I have to come down to the hospital. When I came down to the hospital, there was about 100 like I. And Mengele were there. And there was a Polish doctor, what he was the general doctor from the hospital. He spoke German, too. He was very close with that Mengele, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Mengele gave him the authority he should look over those 100 people, whoever is good still for the camp, and whoever is not good goes to the crematorium. You understand? So they came my line, and so he said, this one there, this one there. When I came in line, the Polish doctor asked me, what's wrong with you? Asked in German language. I know he's a Polack, and I answered him in Polish. So he asked me, are you Polish? I say, of course I'm Polish! I was in the army during the war. I was on the front. I was a war prisoner. And I'm here already four years, I can't take it anymore. I have enough, that life. So he said to me, I'm going to take you to a hospital and I will operate you. And you going to be OK. And he put me on the other side with other few boys what supposed to go to the hospital. And the rest they took on a truck and took them away, right away, to burn. How do you think it was that the doctor picked you to go to the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he started to get feeling for me because I told him how I'm four years survived already down in the-- as a war prisoner, I was fighting for Poland and things. So he got feelings for me. That's what I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the hospital then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the hospital a long time. Because the doctor came in to me, to see me in the room. He gave me a good room. I was in a good room, only heavy, very bad, sick, people. And so he came in and I told him I'm a tailor, if he needs something I will make for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he started to bring me in clothing I should fit it out for him. He got-- took some of the prison clothing, but he wants to be fitted out. So I fit it out. And he said to me, I have to stay at least two or three weeks, I'm going to feel better to get the operation. So in a week, I felt already good, because I was in a warm room, I had food enough. All those people who got the food. There was mostly people who was commandants in the prison, in camp. So he was laying-- they didn't take them no place. They let them lay there. And they couldn't eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought them in food-- baked chickens, I don't know from where-- they brought them in. Not from the camps. Somebody got it from outside for them. He--they couldn't eat. They were after an operation. Do they want me to help them out a little nighttime. I helped them out, and they gave me all the food. And so all my hemorrhoids went in, I was not sick anymore. But now I fear they're going to take me back to work. I look myself, cut off a leg, I shouldn't go back to work. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Mengele see you while you were in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not there. Not before the operation. And then they operated me. He operate me, that Polish doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name? No, I didn't know his name. As far I know, he was killed after the war. They killed him because he did decide who has to go to the crematorium. As far I know. I'm not sure, but somebody told me that. And when did you leave the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: With this operation, then I was in another department after the operation. I was laying there, probably, about six, seven weeks. And Mengele saw me there in that other department after the operation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he told them I should keep me another two weeks because it's cold outside. Can you imagine? [LAUGHS] In the same day he took out 150 people to the crematorium.
